hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Third Conditional and Mixed Conditionals -- Rewriting Outcomes in Postmortems
lesson.menu_desc: After a bad deploy causes a two-hour outage, Ana runs a blameless postmortem: she builds a timeline, finds the root cause and the contributing factors, and rewrites the outcome with third and mixed conditionals -- if we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- Third & Mixed Conditionals
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 16 -- Third & Mixed Conditionals

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a review after an incident to learn from it
a meeting to look back on what went well and badly
understanding a situation only after it has happened
an unplanned event that disrupts a service
a period when a system is down and unavailable
focused on causes, not on blaming people
a key lesson or point to remember

paras: Last month, Ana's remote team had a serious incident: a bad deploy caused a two-hour outage, and users could not log in. The next day, the team ran a postmortem, a calm review of what happened and why. Ana opened with one simple rule: this was a blameless meeting, so they would look at causes, not at people. She wrote a factual timeline first. Then she was honest about the hard truth: if they had tested the new login flow in staging, they would not have shipped the broken build, and the outage would not have happened. The bug was completely preventable.

The root cause was one missing check, but there were two contributing factors as well. An old alert had been switched off months earlier, a small oversight that nobody had noticed. And a similar near miss had happened in the previous sprint, but no one had written it down. In hindsight, the warning signs were all there. Ana used a mixed conditional to make the lesson land: if they had documented that near miss last time, they would not be sitting in this postmortem now. The past mistake was still shaping their present.

The team agreed on three clear takeaways: turn the alert back on, add a staging test for every login change, and log every near miss. Ana was careful, because a postmortem can backfire: if it turns into blame, people hide their mistakes, and the next outage is worse. So she kept it constructive. She even added one more mixed conditional to show it was not simply bad luck: if the team were stricter about staging in general, they would have caught this bug weeks ago. By the end of the retrospective, nobody felt attacked, and everybody had learned something. That, Ana said, is the whole point: you cannot change the outage, but you can change what happens next time.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, incident postmortems and retrospectives

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Ana blames one engineer for the outage and asks the team to be more careful next time.
After an outage, Ana runs a blameless postmortem: she builds a timeline, finds the causes, and turns hindsight into clear, preventable takeaways.
The text is a step-by-step guide to setting up a staging environment from zero.

items: The outage was preventable: if the team had tested the login flow in staging, they would not have shipped the broken build.
t
The text says the bug was 'completely preventable' and that 'if they had tested the new login flow in staging, they would not have shipped the broken build, and the outage would not have happened'.
Ana runs the postmortem to find out which engineer is to blame for the incident.
f
The opposite: 'this was a blameless meeting, so they would look at causes, not at people'.
There was a similar near miss in the previous sprint, but nobody had written it down.
t
The text says 'a similar near miss had happened in the previous sprint, but no one had written it down'.
Ana says a postmortem always makes the next outage worse.
f
She says it CAN backfire only if it turns into blame; she keeps it constructive so people do not hide their mistakes.

gapfill.text: Notice how Ana looks back at the past. THIRD CONDITIONAL talks about a past that did not happen: if + past perfect, would have + past participle. If we had tested it, the outage would not have happened. MIXED CONDITIONALS connect two times: a past cause with a present result (if we had documented it, we would not be here now) or a present truth with a past result (if we were stricter, we would have caught it earlier).

bank.label: Useful sentences for a blameless postmortem
items: If we had caught the bug in staging, the outage would not have happened.
If we had documented the near miss, we would not be dealing with it now.
If our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner.
In hindsight, that oversight was a contributing factor.
This was a preventable incident, so let us treat it as one.
This is a blameless postmortem: we look at causes, not at people.
The main takeaway is to add a staging test for every login change.
If we are not careful, a postmortem can backfire into blame.

lf.title: Look back with the right conditional
items: A
If we
had tested
it in staging, the outage would not have happened. (third conditional)
strong
B
If we had written it down, we
would not be
in this postmortem now. (mixed: past to present)
strong
C
If our monitoring
were better
, we would have caught it sooner. (mixed: present to past)
strong
D
In
hindsight
, the alert being off was a contributing factor. (reflection)
soft

lf.text: Three ways to look back, all built with IF and a WOULD form. THIRD CONDITIONAL: if + past perfect, would have + past participle (a past that did not happen). MIXED, past to present: if + past perfect, would + base verb (a past cause, a present result). MIXED, present to past: if + past simple, would have + past participle (a present truth, a past result). Only the tenses move.

items.situation: A bug reached production because it was never tested in staging. Look back with a third conditional.
tips: If we had tested it in staging, we would not have shipped the bug.
If QA had run the login test, the outage would not have happened.

items.situation: A near miss last sprint was never documented, and now the same problem is back. Use a mixed conditional (past to present).
tips: If we had documented that near miss, we would not be dealing with it again now.
If we had logged it last time, we would already know the fix.

items.situation: Your team is generally too relaxed about staging, and that caused a past bug. Use a mixed conditional (present to past).
tips: If we were stricter about staging, we would have caught this weeks ago.
If our process were tighter, we would not have missed it.

items.situation: State the main takeaway from the postmortem, and warn how it could backfire.
tips: The main takeaway is to test every login change; if we turn this into blame, it will backfire.
Our takeaway is clearer alerts. A blameless review works; a blaming one backfires.

items: Round 1 -- Guided
Describe last month's outage in one third conditional: what the team did not do, and what would not have happened. Speak from your notes.
Round 2 -- Add a mixed conditional
Now connect the past to the present: use 'if we had ...' to explain why the team is dealing with the problem now.
Round 3 -- Run the postmortem
With no notes, give your whole reflection: the incident, one third conditional, one mixed conditional, and a clear, preventable takeaway. Keep it blameless.
Curveball
A teammate says, 'It was just bad luck, nothing we could do.' Answer with a conditional to show the outage was preventable.

answerkey.title: Reveal the postmortem toolkit
list: Vocabulary: postmortem, retrospective, hindsight, incident, outage, blameless, takeaway, oversight, preventable, to backfire, contributing factor, near miss.
Third conditional: 'If we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened.'
Mixed (past to present): 'If we had documented the near miss, we would not be here now.'
Mixed (present to past): 'If our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner.'
Structure: build a timeline, find the causes without blame, and turn hindsight into preventable takeaways.
answerkey.note: A good postmortem is blameless. You cannot change the outage, but a clear conditional turns hindsight into a preventable next step.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, it is David from the platform team. I just read the postmortem you wrote for last week's outage, and honestly, it is one of the clearest I have seen. I have two quick questions before we share it with the wider team. First, you wrote that the outage was preventable, and that if we had tested the login flow in staging, it would not have happened. I completely agree, but should we call the old, switched-off alert the root cause, or just a contributing factor? I want us to be precise. Second, you mentioned a near miss in the previous sprint that nobody logged. Do you think we should add a separate action item just for that, a place to write down near misses, or is that already covered by the new staging test? One more thing: I really liked that this stayed blameless. Nobody felt attacked, and that is exactly why people were honest about what went wrong. Send me two short lines and I will forward it. Thanks, Ana.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I want to give you a quick tip on running postmortems, because yours are getting really strong. The secret is to keep them blameless and to use hindsight well. When you look back, do not say someone should have known. Instead, use a conditional: if we had tested this in staging, the outage would not have happened. That moves the focus from a person to a process. And here is the part people forget: mixed conditionals are your friend in a retrospective. When a past mistake is still hurting you today, say if we had documented that near miss, we would not be dealing with it now. It connects the past cause to the present pain, and it makes the takeaway obvious. One more thing: be careful, because a postmortem can backfire. The moment it feels like blame, people go quiet and hide the next incident. So always separate the cause from the person, find the contributing factors, agree on preventable actions, and let the team leave feeling safe. That is what makes your reviews so easy to trust.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana ran the postmortem after the outage. First, she wrote a factual timeline of the incident, from the first alert to the fix. Then she found the root cause and the contributing factors, without blaming anyone. After that, she used hindsight to see what the team would have done differently. Next, she agreed on preventable actions so the same outage would not happen again. Finally, she shared the key takeaways in a calm, blameless retrospective.
